title: Sausage and chips
subtitle: Grilled pork sausages with oven chips and mushy peas
excerpt: Sausage and chips is the informal British dinner plate that does not need much justification. Good pork sausages cooked until deeply coloured alongside crisp oven chips, with mushy peas or baked beans on the side.
sourceNotes: Standard British weeknight dinner plate, found in school dinners, home kitchens, and casual restaurants. Sausage and chips has been a regular feature of British home cooking since oven-ready chips became widely available in the 1970s.

content.text: Sausage and chips is the plate you make when you want something satisfying without much effort. Cooking the sausages in the oven alongside the chips keeps the washing up to a minimum and means everything is ready at the same time. The sausages need to be turned at least once so they colour on both sides.
Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Spread the chips across one baking tray and place the sausages on a second tray, or on the same tray with some space between them. Put both trays in the oven.
After 12 to 15 minutes, turn the sausages and the chips. Return to the oven for a further 12 to 15 minutes until the sausages are dark golden and cooked through, and the chips are crisp.
While the sausages and chips finish, heat the mushy peas in a small saucepan if using. Serve everything on warm plates.

items.symptom: The sausages are raw inside but brown outside
items.cause: Oven temperature too high, or sausages placed too close to the top element.
items.fix: Cook at 220°C fan on a middle shelf. The sausages need 25 to 30 minutes total. Cut one open to check if in doubt.

items.symptom: The chips are soft and pale
items.cause: Overcrowded tray, or the chips had frost on them when they went in.
items.fix: Spread chips in a single layer. If the bag is heavily iced, break up any clumps before spreading.

items.symptom: The sausages split and dry out
items.cause: Temperature too high or cooked too long.
items.fix: Do not cook above 220°C fan and start checking at the 25-minute mark.

content.text: Sausage and chips is a meal the British treat as a reliable fallback rather than a feature, which means it gets cooked far more often than it gets discussed. The plate is found in school dinner halls, pub menus under the heading of "traditional," and in home kitchens on the nights when nobody wants to think about what to make. It is better than it gets credit for when the sausages are good.
